chapter_title: The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes solutions in the amazing world of solutes, solvents, and solutions?

options.text: The solute is the substance that dissolves in a solution.
A solution is a homogeneous mixture in which one or more substances are uniformly distributed in another.
The solvent is the component that dissolves the solute and usually forms the larger part of a solution.
Solubility is the ability of a substance to dissolve in a particular solvent under specified conditions.

questions.explanation: A solution is a homogeneous mixture in which one or more substances are uniformly distributed in another.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with solutions?

options.text: Water is the solvent in sugar water.
Salt is the solute when it dissolves in water.
Salt water is a solution when the salt has dissolved completely.
Sugar is soluble in water under ordinary conditions.

questions.explanation: Salt water is a solution when the salt has dissolved completely.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes solutions from a nearby idea?

options.text: Solubility depends on the pair of substances and conditions, not on the solute alone.
Solute describes the dissolved component, not necessarily the component present in the smaller amount in every case.
Solvent refers to the dissolving medium, not to every liquid in a container.
A solution is a mixture even though it looks uniform.

questions.explanation: A solution is a mixture even though it looks uniform.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of solutions?

options.text: Identify the components of a familiar solution before discussing its concentration.
Label the dissolved substance when drawing or preparing a solution.
Choose a solvent based on whether it can dissolve the substance safely.
Test equal amounts with a fixed solvent volume and record whether they dissolve.

questions.explanation: Identify the components of a familiar solution before discussing its concentration.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about solutions?

options.text: The solute does not vanish; its particles spread through the solvent.
A clear liquid is not automatically a solution.
Water is a common solvent but not the solvent for every solution.
A substance being soluble in water does not mean it dissolves in every solvent.

questions.explanation: A clear liquid is not automatically a solution.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A learner is explaining solutions. Which description is most accurate?

options.text: The solvent is the component that dissolves the solute and usually forms the larger part of a solution.
The solute is the substance that dissolves in a solution.
A solution is a homogeneous mixture in which one or more substances are uniformly distributed in another.
Solubility is the ability of a substance to dissolve in a particular solvent under specified conditions.

questions.explanation: A solution is a homogeneous mixture in which one or more substances are uniformly distributed in another.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which observation would provide useful evidence about solutions?

options.text: Sugar is soluble in water under ordinary conditions.
Salt is the solute when it dissolves in water.
Water is the solvent in sugar water.
Salt water is a solution when the salt has dissolved completely.

questions.explanation: Salt water is a solution when the salt has dissolved completely.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

options.text: Identify the components of a familiar solution before discussing its concentration.
Label the dissolved substance when drawing or preparing a solution.

Test equal amounts with a fixed solvent volume and record whether they dissolve.

questions.explanation: Identify the components of a familiar solution before discussing its concentration.

options.text: Solute describes the dissolved component, not necessarily the component present in the smaller amount in every case.
A solution is a mixture even though it looks uniform.

questions.explanation: A solution is a mixture even though it looks uniform.

questions.question_text: Which plan would best investigate or demonstrate solutions?

options.text: Choose a solvent based on whether it can dissolve the substance safely.
Label the dissolved substance when drawing or preparing a solution.
Identify the components of a familiar solution before discussing its concentration.
Test equal amounts with a fixed solvent volume and record whether they dissolve.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes solute in the amazing world of solutes, solvents, and solutions?

options.text: An insoluble substance does not dissolve appreciably in a particular solvent under the stated conditions.
The solvent is the component that dissolves the solute and usually forms the larger part of a solution.
Solubility is the ability of a substance to dissolve in a particular solvent under specified conditions.
The solute is the substance that dissolves in a solution.

questions.explanation: The solute is the substance that dissolves in a solution.

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with solute?

options.text: Salt is the solute when it dissolves in water.
Water is the solvent in sugar water.

questions.explanation: Salt is the solute when it dissolves in water.

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes solute from a nearby idea?

options.text: Solvent refers to the dissolving medium, not to every liquid in a container.
Solute describes the dissolved component, not necessarily the component present in the smaller amount in every case.
Solubility depends on the pair of substances and conditions, not on the solute alone.
Insoluble means very little dissolves in that solvent and condition, not that the substance can never dissolve anywhere.

questions.explanation: Solute describes the dissolved component, not necessarily the component present in the smaller amount in every case.

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of solute?

options.text: Test equal amounts with a fixed solvent volume and record whether they dissolve.
Choose a solvent based on whether it can dissolve the substance safely.
Label the dissolved substance when drawing or preparing a solution.
Use filtration to separate an insoluble solid from water.

questions.explanation: Label the dissolved substance when drawing or preparing a solution.

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about solute?

options.text: Cloudiness or suspension is not evidence that the solid has dissolved.
Water is a common solvent but not the solvent for every solution.
A substance being soluble in water does not mean it dissolves in every solvent.
The solute does not vanish; its particles spread through the solvent.

questions.explanation: The solute does not vanish; its particles spread through the solvent.

options.text: Label the dissolved substance when drawing or preparing a solution.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes solvent in the amazing world of solutes, solvents, and solutions?

options.text: Solubility is the ability of a substance to dissolve in a particular solvent under specified conditions.
The solvent is the component that dissolves the solute and usually forms the larger part of a solution.
An insoluble substance does not dissolve appreciably in a particular solvent under the stated conditions.
Concentration compares the amount of solute with the amount of solution or solvent used.

questions.explanation: The solvent is the component that dissolves the solute and usually forms the larger part of a solution.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with solvent?

options.text: Sand is insoluble in water.
Sugar is soluble in water under ordinary conditions.
Water is the solvent in sugar water.
A spoonful of sugar in a small cup is more concentrated than the same spoonful in a large jug.

questions.explanation: Water is the solvent in sugar water.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes solvent from a nearby idea?

options.text: Concentration depends on both solute and the chosen volume or mass basis.
Solubility depends on the pair of substances and conditions, not on the solute alone.
Insoluble means very little dissolves in that solvent and condition, not that the substance can never dissolve anywhere.
Solvent refers to the dissolving medium, not to every liquid in a container.

questions.explanation: Solvent refers to the dissolving medium, not to every liquid in a container.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of solvent?

options.text: Choose a solvent based on whether it can dissolve the substance safely.
Test equal amounts with a fixed solvent volume and record whether they dissolve.
Use filtration to separate an insoluble solid from water.
Keep the solvent volume fixed when comparing how much solute is added.

questions.explanation: Choose a solvent based on whether it can dissolve the substance safely.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about solvent?

options.text: A substance being soluble in water does not mean it dissolves in every solvent.
Water is a common solvent but not the solvent for every solution.
Cloudiness or suspension is not evidence that the solid has dissolved.
A larger total volume does not automatically mean a more concentrated solution.

questions.explanation: Water is a common solvent but not the solvent for every solution.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes solubility in the amazing world of solutes, solvents, and solutions?

options.text: A saturated solution contains as much dissolved solute as it can under specified conditions.
An insoluble substance does not dissolve appreciably in a particular solvent under the stated conditions.
Concentration compares the amount of solute with the amount of solution or solvent used.
Solubility is the ability of a substance to dissolve in a particular solvent under specified conditions.

questions.explanation: Solubility is the ability of a substance to dissolve in a particular solvent under specified conditions.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with solubility?

options.text: Sugar is soluble in water under ordinary conditions.
Sand is insoluble in water.
A spoonful of sugar in a small cup is more concentrated than the same spoonful in a large jug.
Extra salt remains at the bottom after a saturated salt solution is stirred.

questions.explanation: Sugar is soluble in water under ordinary conditions.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes solubility from a nearby idea?

options.text: Insoluble means very little dissolves in that solvent and condition, not that the substance can never dissolve anywhere.
Solubility depends on the pair of substances and conditions, not on the solute alone.
Concentration depends on both solute and the chosen volume or mass basis.
Saturation is a condition at a particular temperature and amount of solvent.

questions.explanation: Solubility depends on the pair of substances and conditions, not on the solute alone.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of solubility?

options.text: Keep the solvent volume fixed when comparing how much solute is added.
Use filtration to separate an insoluble solid from water.
Test equal amounts with a fixed solvent volume and record whether they dissolve.
Add solute gradually and record the point at which undissolved material remains.

questions.explanation: Test equal amounts with a fixed solvent volume and record whether they dissolve.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about solubility?

options.text: A saturated solution is not necessarily the most concentrated solution possible under all conditions.
Cloudiness or suspension is not evidence that the solid has dissolved.
A larger total volume does not automatically mean a more concentrated solution.
A substance being soluble in water does not mean it dissolves in every solvent.

questions.explanation: A substance being soluble in water does not mean it dissolves in every solvent.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes insoluble substances in the amazing world of solutes, solvents, and solutions?

options.text: Concentration compares the amount of solute with the amount of solution or solvent used.
An insoluble substance does not dissolve appreciably in a particular solvent under the stated conditions.
A saturated solution contains as much dissolved solute as it can under specified conditions.
Changing temperature can change how much of a substance dissolves, depending on the substance and solvent.

questions.explanation: An insoluble substance does not dissolve appreciably in a particular solvent under the stated conditions.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with insoluble substances?

options.text: Extra salt remains at the bottom after a saturated salt solution is stirred.
A spoonful of sugar in a small cup is more concentrated than the same spoonful in a large jug.
Sand is insoluble in water.
More sugar generally dissolves in warm water than in the same amount of cold water.

questions.explanation: Sand is insoluble in water.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes insoluble substances from a nearby idea?

options.text: The effect of temperature is not identical for every solute.
Concentration depends on both solute and the chosen volume or mass basis.
Saturation is a condition at a particular temperature and amount of solvent.
Insoluble means very little dissolves in that solvent and condition, not that the substance can never dissolve anywhere.

questions.explanation: Insoluble means very little dissolves in that solvent and condition, not that the substance can never dissolve anywhere.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of insoluble substances?

options.text: Use filtration to separate an insoluble solid from water.
Keep the solvent volume fixed when comparing how much solute is added.
Add solute gradually and record the point at which undissolved material remains.
Compare equal solvent volumes at controlled temperatures and measure the dissolved amount.

questions.explanation: Use filtration to separate an insoluble solid from water.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about insoluble substances?

options.text: A larger total volume does not automatically mean a more concentrated solution.
Cloudiness or suspension is not evidence that the solid has dissolved.
A saturated solution is not necessarily the most concentrated solution possible under all conditions.
Stirring and temperature are different variables and should not be confused.

questions.explanation: Cloudiness or suspension is not evidence that the solid has dissolved.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A learner is explaining insoluble substances. Which description is most accurate?

options.text: A saturated solution contains as much dissolved solute as it can under specified conditions.
Concentration compares the amount of solute with the amount of solution or solvent used.
An insoluble substance does not dissolve appreciably in a particular solvent under the stated conditions.
Changing temperature can change how much of a substance dissolves, depending on the substance and solvent.

Sand is insoluble in water.

questions.question_text: Which plan would best investigate or demonstrate insoluble substances?

options.text: Add solute gradually and record the point at which undissolved material remains.
Keep the solvent volume fixed when comparing how much solute is added.
Use filtration to separate an insoluble solid from water.
Compare equal solvent volumes at controlled temperatures and measure the dissolved amount.

questions.explanation: Use filtration to separate an insoluble solid from water.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes concentration in the amazing world of solutes, solvents, and solutions?

options.text: A suspension contains particles dispersed in a fluid that may settle and can often be filtered.
A saturated solution contains as much dissolved solute as it can under specified conditions.
Changing temperature can change how much of a substance dissolves, depending on the substance and solvent.
Concentration compares the amount of solute with the amount of solution or solvent used.

questions.explanation: Concentration compares the amount of solute with the amount of solution or solvent used.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with concentration?

options.text: A spoonful of sugar in a small cup is more concentrated than the same spoonful in a large jug.
Extra salt remains at the bottom after a saturated salt solution is stirred.
More sugar generally dissolves in warm water than in the same amount of cold water.
Mud in water forms a suspension.

questions.explanation: A spoonful of sugar in a small cup is more concentrated than the same spoonful in a large jug.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes concentration from a nearby idea?

options.text: Saturation is a condition at a particular temperature and amount of solvent.
Concentration depends on both solute and the chosen volume or mass basis.
The effect of temperature is not identical for every solute.
Suspension particles are not uniformly dissolved like solute particles in a solution.

questions.explanation: Concentration depends on both solute and the chosen volume or mass basis.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of concentration?

options.text: Compare equal solvent volumes at controlled temperatures and measure the dissolved amount.
Add solute gradually and record the point at which undissolved material remains.
Keep the solvent volume fixed when comparing how much solute is added.
Allow settling or filter a suspension to separate its dispersed solid.

questions.explanation: Keep the solvent volume fixed when comparing how much solute is added.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about concentration?

options.text: A liquid that looks cloudy is not necessarily a solution.
A saturated solution is not necessarily the most concentrated solution possible under all conditions.
Stirring and temperature are different variables and should not be confused.
A larger total volume does not automatically mean a more concentrated solution.

questions.explanation: A larger total volume does not automatically mean a more concentrated solution.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

A suspension contains particles dispersed in a fluid that may settle and can often be filtered.

questions.explanation: Concentration compares the amount of solute with the amount of solution or solvent used.

Concentration depends on both solute and the chosen volume or mass basis.

Allow settling or filter a suspension to separate its dispersed solid.

questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes saturated solutions in the amazing world of solutes, solvents, and solutions?

options.text: Changing temperature can change how much of a substance dissolves, depending on the substance and solvent.
A saturated solution contains as much dissolved solute as it can under specified conditions.
A suspension contains particles dispersed in a fluid that may settle and can often be filtered.
Crystallisation forms solid crystals from a solution by removing some solvent or changing conditions.

questions.explanation: A saturated solution contains as much dissolved solute as it can under specified conditions.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 9; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with saturated solutions?

options.text: Mud in water forms a suspension.
More sugar generally dissolves in warm water than in the same amount of cold water.
Extra salt remains at the bottom after a saturated salt solution is stirred.
Salt crystals can form when a salt solution loses water by evaporation.

questions.explanation: Extra salt remains at the bottom after a saturated salt solution is stirred.

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes saturated solutions from a nearby idea?

options.text: Crystallisation can recover a dissolved solid that filtration alone cannot catch.
The effect of temperature is not identical for every solute.

Saturation is a condition at a particular temperature and amount of solvent.

questions.explanation: Saturation is a condition at a particular temperature and amount of solvent.

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of saturated solutions?

options.text: Add solute gradually and record the point at which undissolved material remains.
Compare equal solvent volumes at controlled temperatures and measure the dissolved amount.
Allow settling or filter a suspension to separate its dispersed solid.
Evaporate carefully and stop before the recovered crystals are contaminated or overheated.

questions.explanation: Add solute gradually and record the point at which undissolved material remains.

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about saturated solutions?
